author: Tertullian
book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.

The Session of Jesus in His Incarnate Nature at the Right Hand of God a Guarantee of the Resurrection of Our Flesh.

ccel.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

title: The Session of Jesus in His Incarnate Nature at the Right Hand of God a Guarantee of the Resurrection of Our Flesh.

source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: That, however, which we have reserved for a concluding argument, will now stand as a plea for all, and for the apostle himself, who in very deed would have to be charged with extreme indiscretion, if he had so abruptly, as some will have it, and as they say, blindfold, and so indiscriminately, and so unconditionally, excluded from the kingdom of God, and indeed from the court of heaven itself, all flesh and blood whatsoever; since Jesus is still sitting there at the right hand of the Father, man, yet God—the last Adam, yet the primary Word—flesh and blood, yet purer than ours—who “shall descend in like manner as He ascended into heaven” the same both in substance and form, as the angels affirmed, so as even to be recognised by those who pierced Him.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Designated, as He is, “the Mediator between God and man,” He keeps in His own self the deposit of the flesh which has been committed to Him by both parties—the pledge and security of its entire perfection.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For as “He has given to us the earnest of the Spirit,” so has He received from us the earnest of the flesh, and has carried it with Him into heaven as a pledge of that complete entirety which is one day to be restored to it.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Be not disquieted, O flesh and blood, with any care; in Christ you have acquired both heaven and the kingdom of God.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Otherwise, if they say that you are not in Christ, let them also say that Christ is not in heaven, since they have denied you heaven.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Likewise “neither shall corruption,” says he, “inherit incorruption.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: This he says, not that you may take flesh and blood to be corruption, for they are themselves rather the subjects of corruption,—I mean through death, since death does not so much corrupt, as actually consume, our flesh and blood.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But inasmuch as he had plainly said that the works of the flesh and blood could not obtain the kingdom of God, with the view of stating this with accumulated stress, he deprived corruption itself—that is, death, which profits so largely by the works of the flesh and blood—from all inheritance of incorruption.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For a little afterwards, he has described what is, as it were, the death of death itself: “Death,” says he, “is swallowed up in victory.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: O death, where is thy sting?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: O grave, where is thy victory?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: The sting of death is sin”—here is the corruption; “and the strength of sin is the law”—that other law, no doubt, which he has described “in his members as warring against the law of his mind,”—meaning, of course, the actual power of sinning against his will.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now he says in a previous passage (of our Epistle to the Corinthians), that “the last enemy to be destroyed is death.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: In this way, then, it is that corruption shall not inherit incorruption; in other words, death shall not continue.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: When and how shall it cease?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: In that “moment, that twinkling of an eye, at the last trump, when the dead shall rise incorruptible.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But what are these, if not they who were corruptible before—that is, our bodies; in other words, our flesh and blood?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: And we undergo the change.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But in what condition, if not in that wherein we shall be found?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: “For this corruptible must put on incorruption, and this mortal must put on immortality.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: What mortal is this but the flesh? what corruptible but the blood.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Moreover, that you may not suppose the apostle to have any other meaning, in his care to teach you, and that you may understand him seriously to apply his statement to the flesh, when he says “this corruptible” and “this mortal,” he utters the words while touching the surface of his own body.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: He certainly could not have pronounced these phrases except in reference to an object which was palpable and apparent.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: The expression indicates a bodily exhibition.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Moreover, a corruptible body is one thing, and corruption is another; so a mortal body is one thing, and mortality is another.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For that which suffers is one thing, and that which causes it to suffer is another.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Consequently, those things which are subject to corruption and mortality, even the flesh and blood, must needs also be susceptible of incorruption and immortality.
